title: Introduction to Vectors: Geometric Vectors and Their Properties
methodology: DeepSeek-first analysis + DeepSeek quality repair + deterministic validator/renderer QA

lessonAnalysis.topic: Introduction to geometric vectors: definition, magnitude, direction, equal vectors, opposite vectors, parallel vectors, and bearing representation.
lessonAnalysis.coreLearningGoal: Understand that a vector has both magnitude and direction, and be able to identify equal, opposite, and parallel vectors from diagrams and descriptions.
successCriteria: Define a vector as a quantity with magnitude and direction.
Identify equal vectors (same magnitude and direction).
Identify opposite vectors (same magnitude, opposite direction).
Identify parallel vectors (same or opposite direction).
Represent direction using compass (N/S/E/W) and true bearing (clockwise from north).
Calculate magnitude from components using Pythagorean theorem.
lessonExamplesDetected: Cube example: vectors EF, AB, GC, etc. on a cube with side length 10 cm.
Map example: distance from Toronto to Mississauga (approx 15 km) with scale.
Velocity example: 3 m/s east and 4 m/s north gives resultant 5 m/s.
Force example: two boxes pushed with 5 N east each, total 10 N east.
Grid example: vectors PR and RQ on a square grid.
targetProducts: Drawing vectors on a cube and identifying equal/opposite pairs.
Identifying true/false statements about parallel and opposite vectors.
inputEvidenceStudentsShouldUse: Grid with points P, Q, R.
doNotRevealAsQuestionCondition: The resultant velocity magnitude (5 m/s) should not be given as a condition; students should compute it.
The total force (10 N east) should not be given; students should add vectors.

problemAnchor: A delivery drone flies 3 km east then 4 km north. What is its displacement from the start?

abt.and: Vectors have both magnitude and direction, and we can represent them as arrows.
abt.but: Many students confuse magnitude with direction, or think vectors are just numbers.
abt.therefore: We need to practice identifying equal, opposite, and parallel vectors, and using bearings to describe direction.

objective: By the end of this exit card, you will be able to identify and describe geometric vectors, including their magnitude, direction, and relationships (equal, opposite, parallel).

knowledgeNodes.name: Definition of a vector
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher states: 'Vectors are quantities that have both magnitude and direction.'

knowledgeNodes.name: Magnitude of a vector
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher explains magnitude as length, always positive, and uses Pythagorean theorem: sqrt(3^2+4^2)=5.

knowledgeNodes.name: Direction of a vector
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher discusses compass directions (N/S/E/W) and true bearing (clockwise from north).

knowledgeNodes.name: Equal vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher defines equal vectors as having same magnitude and direction, uses cube example: EF = AB.

knowledgeNodes.name: Opposite vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher defines opposite vectors as same magnitude, opposite direction, uses cube example: FB and AE.

knowledgeNodes.name: Parallel vectors
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher explains parallel vectors have same or opposite direction, can be placed on same line.

knowledgeNodes.name: Scalar vs vector
knowledgeNodes.lessonEvidence: Teacher contrasts scalars (only magnitude) with vectors (magnitude and direction).

errorTaxonomy.meaning: Confuses magnitude with direction; thinks a vector is defined only by its length.
Thinks equal vectors must be on the same line or have same starting point.
Thinks opposite vectors are not parallel.
Misapplies Pythagorean theorem or forgets to take square root when computing magnitude.
Confuses true bearing (clockwise from north) with compass quadrant bearing.
Thinks all parallel vectors are equal.
Cannot identify vector direction from diagram (tail to head).

figures.title: Comparison of vectors u, v, w, z
Grid with points P, Q, R
Two boxes pushed east with 5 N each

stemParts.value: Which of the following quantities is a vector?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Temperature has only magnitude, so it is a scalar.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Mass has only magnitude, so it is a scalar.

options.label: C
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Velocity has both magnitude (20 m/s) and direction (north).

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Time has only magnitude, so it is a scalar.

stemParts.value: A vector has components 3 units east and 4 units north. What is its magnitude?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. You added the components directly. Use the Pythagorean theorem: sqrt(3^2+4^2)=5.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. You multiplied the components. Use the Pythagorean theorem: sqrt(3^2+4^2)=5.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. You forgot to take the square root. sqrt(3^2+4^2)=5, not 25.

stemParts.value: In the figure, which vector is equal to vector u?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Both u and v have the same magnitude and direction (rightward).

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. w points left, while u points right.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. z points left, while u points right.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. v is equal to u.

stemParts.value: In the figure, which vector is opposite to vector u?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. v points same direction as u, not opposite.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! w points left, opposite to u's rightward direction, same magnitude.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. z points left but has different magnitude than u.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. w is opposite to u.

stemParts.value: True or False: If two vectors are parallel, they must be equal.

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. Parallel vectors can have different magnitudes. For example, two vectors pointing east with lengths 3 and 5 are parallel but not equal.

options.label: B
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Parallel vectors have the same or opposite direction but may have different magnitudes.

options.label: C
options.text: Only if they point the same direction
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. Even if they point the same direction, they may have different magnitudes.

options.label: D
options.text: Only if they have the same magnitude
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. They also need the same direction to be equal.

stemParts.value: Using the compass rose in the figure, what is the true bearing of the vector pointing northeast?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Northeast is 45° clockwise from north, so true bearing is 045°.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. 135° is southeast.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. 225° is southwest.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. 315° is northwest.

stemParts.value: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?

options.label: A
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. Displacement has direction, so it is a vector.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. Force has direction, so it is a vector.

options.label: C
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! Speed has only magnitude, so it is a scalar.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. Acceleration has direction, so it is a vector.

stemParts.value: In the grid figure, vector PR is drawn from P to R. What is the direction of vector PR?

options.label: A
options.feedbackCorrect: Correct! PR goes from P (top) to R (bottom), so it is downward.

options.label: B
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. PR goes from P to R, which is downward.

options.label: C
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. PR is vertical, not horizontal.

options.label: D
options.feedbackWrong: Incorrect. PR is vertical, not horizontal.

stemParts.value: A vector has components 6 units west and 8 units south. Calculate its magnitude. Show your work.

questions.rubric: Correct answer: 10 units. Work: sqrt(6^2+8^2)=sqrt(36+64)=sqrt(100)=10. Accept 10 units or 10.

stemParts.value: A ship sails 50 km on a true bearing of 120°. Describe its direction using compass quadrants (e.g., N30°E).

questions.rubric: Correct: S60°E (since 120° clockwise from north is 60° east of south). Accept equivalent descriptions like '60° east of south' or 'S60°E'.
questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The direction is S60°E, meaning 60° east of south.

reteachModules.trigger: Student selects scalar when asked for vector or vice versa.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Thinks a vector is defined only by its magnitude or confuses vector with scalar.

miniLesson.explain: A vector has both magnitude and direction. A scalar has only magnitude. For example, speed (scalar) vs velocity (vector).
miniLesson.example: Temperature 25°C is scalar; wind 20 km/h north is vector.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Think of an arrow: the length is magnitude, the arrowhead shows direction.

guidedPractice.prompt: Is '5 kg' a vector or scalar?
Is 'displacement 10 m east' a vector or scalar?
Give an example of a vector and a scalar from everyday life.
guidedPractice.answer: Vector: force 5 N up; scalar: time 10 s.

reteachModules.trigger: Student thinks equal vectors must start at same point or be on same line.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Confuses equal vectors with identical vectors (same position).

miniLesson.explain: Equal vectors have the same magnitude and direction, regardless of starting point. They are parallel and point the same way.
miniLesson.example: In the figure, vectors u and v are equal: both same length, both pointing right.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Equal vectors are like two arrows of same length pointing same way, even if placed elsewhere.

guidedPractice.prompt: Are two vectors equal if they have same length but different direction?
In the figure, is vector u equal to vector w? (u right, w left)
Draw two equal vectors that are not on the same line.
guidedPractice.answer: Two parallel arrows of same length pointing same direction, separated.

reteachModules.trigger: Student says opposite vectors are not parallel.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Thinks parallel means same direction only.

miniLesson.explain: Parallel vectors can be in the same or opposite direction. Opposite vectors are always parallel because they lie on parallel lines.
miniLesson.example: Vectors u and w in the figure are opposite and parallel.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Parallel lines can go same way or opposite ways; vectors on them are parallel.

guidedPractice.prompt: True or false: Opposite vectors are parallel.
Give an example of two parallel vectors that are not opposite.
guidedPractice.answer: Two vectors pointing east of different lengths.

guidedPractice.prompt: If vector u and v are opposite, are they parallel? Explain.
guidedPractice.answer: Yes, because they lie on parallel lines.

reteachModules.trigger: Student computes magnitude incorrectly (e.g., adds components or forgets square root).

miniLesson.explain: Magnitude = sqrt(x^2 + y^2). Do not add components directly; square each, sum, then take square root.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Pythagorean theorem: a^2+b^2=c^2, so c = sqrt(a^2+b^2).

guidedPractice.prompt: Find magnitude of vector with components 5 and 12.
Find magnitude of vector with components 8 and 15.
A vector has components 2 and 2. What is its magnitude? Simplify.

reteachModules.trigger: Student confuses true bearing with quadrant bearing.
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Thinks bearing is measured from east or counterclockwise.

miniLesson.explain: True bearing is measured clockwise from north, always three digits (e.g., 045°). Quadrant bearing uses N/S and E/W (e.g., N45°E).
miniLesson.example: Northeast is 045° true bearing, or N45°E quadrant.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: True bearing: start at north, turn clockwise. Like a compass rose: north=000°, east=090°, south=180°, west=270°.

guidedPractice.prompt: What is the true bearing of east?
Convert S30°W to true bearing.
A vector points 60° east of north. What is its true bearing?

reteachModules.trigger: Student thinks all parallel vectors are equal.

miniLesson.explain: Parallel vectors have same or opposite direction, but may have different magnitudes. Equal vectors must have same magnitude AND direction.
miniLesson.example: Two arrows pointing east, one 5 cm, one 10 cm: parallel but not equal.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Parallel = same line direction; equal = same length AND same direction.

guidedPractice.prompt: Are all parallel vectors equal?
Give an example of parallel vectors that are not equal.
guidedPractice.answer: Vector of length 2 east and vector of length 3 east.

guidedPractice.prompt: If two vectors are equal, must they be parallel? Explain.
guidedPractice.answer: Yes, because same direction implies parallel.

reteachModules.trigger: Student misidentifies direction from diagram (e.g., reads tail to head incorrectly).
reteachModules.studentMisunderstanding: Confuses which end is tail and which is head.

miniLesson.explain: A vector is drawn from tail to head. The arrowhead points to the head. The direction is from tail to head.
miniLesson.example: In vector AB, A is tail, B is head, direction from A to B.
miniLesson.memoryAnchor: Read vector notation: first letter is tail, second is head. Arrow points from first to second.

guidedPractice.prompt: In vector PQ, which point is the tail?
If vector is drawn from X to Y, what is the direction?
Draw vector from (0,0) to (3,4). What is its direction?

questions.stem: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
Explain the difference between a scalar and a vector. Give one example of each.
questions.rubric: Scalar has only magnitude (e.g., mass). Vector has magnitude and direction (e.g., displacement).

questions.stem: Two vectors are equal if they have:
Draw two equal vectors that are not on the same line. Describe them.
questions.rubric: Two arrows of same length pointing same direction, not overlapping.

questions.stem: Explain why opposite vectors are parallel.
questions.rubric: They lie on parallel lines (same orientation, opposite direction).

questions.stem: A vector has components 9 and 12. Its magnitude is:
Find the magnitude of a vector with components 7 and 24. Show work.
The true bearing of a vector pointing directly west is:
Convert the quadrant bearing N60°W to a true bearing.
If two vectors are parallel, they:
Give an example of two parallel vectors that are not equal.
questions.rubric: e.g., 3 cm east and 5 cm east.

questions.stem: In vector notation, vector AB is drawn from:
Draw a vector from point (1,2) to point (4,6). What is its direction (angle from horizontal)?

teacherEscalation.condition: Student scores 0-4 correct on diagnostic, or shows persistent confusion on multiple error types after reteach.
teacherEscalation.message: Please schedule a 1-on-1 session with Michael to review vector fundamentals, especially the definition of vectors, magnitude, and direction.
